audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 9, first-pass restored draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for servant agency, women at well, gift/custom issues, seer terminology, private revelation, Saul's body description, Benjamin status, royal-leader vocabulary, and anti-leader-cult safeguards.

verses.restored: There was a man of Benjamin whose name was Kish son of Abiel, son of Zeror, son of Becorath, son of Aphiah, a Benjaminite, a man of standing.
He had a son whose name was Saul, a choice and handsome young man; no Israelite was more handsome than he, and from his shoulders upward he was taller than all the people.
The female donkeys of Kish, Saul's father, were lost. Kish said to Saul his son, Take, please, one of the servants with you, arise, and go seek the donkeys.

He passed through hill country of Ephraim and through land of Shalishah, but they did not find them. They passed through land of Shaalim, and they were not there; he passed through land of Benjamin, but they did not find them.
They came to land of Zuph, and Saul said to his servant who was with him, Come, let us return, lest my father stop caring about donkeys and become anxious about us.

He said to him, Look, please, a man of Divine is in this city, and the man is honored; all that he says surely comes to pass. Now let us go there; perhaps he will tell us our way upon which we have gone.
Saul said to his servant, But look, if we go, what shall we bring the man? Bread is gone from our vessels, and there is no gift to bring to man of Divine. What is with us?
The servant answered Saul again and said, Look, there is found in my hand a quarter shekel of silver. I will give it to man of Divine, and he will tell us our way.
Formerly in Israel, thus a person said when going to inquire of Divine: Come, let us go to seer; for prophet of today was formerly called seer.
Saul said to his servant, Your word is good; come, let us go. And they went to city where man of Divine was.
As they were going up ascent to city, they found young women going out to draw water and said to them, Is seer here?
They answered them and said, He is; look, he is before you. Hurry now, for today he came to city because people have a sacrifice today at high place.

As you enter city, you will find him before he goes up to high place to eat. People will not eat until he comes, for he blesses sacrifice; afterward those invited eat. Now go up, for about this time you will find him.
They went up to city. As they entered within city, look, Samuel was coming out toward them to go up to high place.
Now YHWH had uncovered Samuel's ear one day before Saul came, saying,

About this time tomorrow I will send to you a man from land of Benjamin. You shall anoint him as leader over my people Israel, and he shall save my people from hand of Philistines, for I have seen my people because their cry has come to me.
When Samuel saw Saul, YHWH answered him, Look, the man of whom I spoke to you; this one shall restrain/govern my people.
Saul approached Samuel in middle of gate and said, Tell me, please, where house of seer is.
Samuel answered Saul and said, I am seer. Go up before me to high place, and you shall eat with me today. In morning I will send you away, and all that is in your heart I will tell you.
As for donkeys lost to you three days ago, do not set your heart on them, for they have been found. And toward whom is all desire of Israel? Is it not toward you and toward all house of your father?
Saul answered and said, Am I not a Benjaminite, from smallest of tribes of Israel, and my clan smallest of all clans of tribe of Benjamin? Why then have you spoken to me a word like this?
Samuel took Saul and his servant, brought them into hall, and gave them a place at head of invited guests, about thirty persons.
Samuel said to cook, Give portion I gave you, of which I said to you, Set it beside you.

The cook lifted thigh and what was upon it and set it before Saul. Samuel said, Look, what was kept is set before you. Eat, because it was kept for you for appointed time, since I said, I have invited people. Saul ate with Samuel that day.
They came down from high place to city, and he spoke with Saul upon roof.
They arose early. At break of dawn Samuel called to Saul upon roof, saying, Rise, and I will send you away. Saul arose, and the two of them, he and Samuel, went outside.
As they were going down at edge of city, Samuel said to Saul, Say to servant that he pass before us—and he passed on—but you stand now, and I will cause you to hear word of Divine.

translation_notes: Saul begins not as a conqueror but as a son searching for lost animals with a servant whose practical knowledge redirects the journey.
The servant is an active guide and financial contributor; class status does not make his insight secondary.
The young women at the well provide essential public information and are not romanticized or treated as scenery.
'Seer' and 'prophet' reflect historical terminology; spiritual authority must not be bought, although customary gifts supported consultation.
Saul's height and appearance are narrative features, not proof of fitness, masculinity, Divine favor, or leadership worth.
The verb in verse 17 can carry restrain, govern, or hold back; kingship enters with ambiguity rather than uncomplicated celebration.
Anointing is presented as response to the people's cry under Philistine pressure. It does not place Saul beyond accountability.
Benjamin's smallness and Saul's self-description must not support ethnic hierarchy, false humility tests, or leader cults.
safety_note: Do not turn physical attractiveness, height, ancestry, private revelation, ceremonial seating, or anointing into proof that a leader deserves obedience. Do not erase servants or women whose knowledge enables the story. Prophetic consultation may not be commercialized into spiritual exploitation.
